LESSON  F.3
Lesson F.3 — Interactive

Guidance, Navigation & Control (GNC)

If the EPS is the heartbeat of the spacecraft, GNC is the nervous system. It tells the spacecraft where it is, which way it is pointing, and how to get where it needs to go.

Real hardware — explore the photo

Tap the numbered markers to identify what you are looking at.
REAL HARDWARE

Europa Clipper's reaction wheels during assembly at JPL

All four flight reaction wheels mounted to NASA's Europa Clipper spacecraft structure during assembly at JPL. Tap the numbered markers to explore the hardware.

NASA/JPL-Caltech

GNC critical components

Click each component to see what it does and what ATLO verifies.

What GNC Does

Guidance determines where the spacecraft needs to go and what trajectory to follow. Navigation determines where the spacecraft actually is and how it is oriented right now. Control executes the commands needed to get from current state to desired state -- firing thrusters, spinning reaction wheels, adjusting attitude.

What Makes GNC Different

Most subsystem anomalies look like the problem they are. GNC anomalies are different -- they can look like mechanical, software, or power problems before revealing themselves as GNC issues. Cross-subsystem awareness is more important with GNC than almost any other subsystem.

KEY POINT

Before running a GNC functional test procedure, ask the GNC engineer to walk you through what each test is verifying. Understanding the expected outcome is what allows you to catch anomalies. You do not need to fully understand the math -- but you need to know what "normal" looks like.
